coredata.dc:title: Zinc and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as green and efficient anticorrosion and antibacterial inhibitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Zhiji
Huang, Weilong
Guo, Changgui
Zhong, Huanglian
Liu, Lin
Zhang, Pengfei
Tan, Tianhui
Zou, Mingang
Zheng, Shiyu
Ye, Xinyun
Ye, Y.W.
Jiang, Qiuhua
coredata.dc:description: A green and efficient method was used to synthesize zinc and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Zn-N-CDs) using ammonium citrate and zinc gluconate as raw materials. A systematic and in-depth study was conducted on the microstructure and protective behaviors of Zn-N-CDs. Results revealed that the impedance modulus (|Z|0.01 Hz) and inhibition efficiency (IE) showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing with the increase of Zn/N ratio, achieving the highest values of 4727 Ω cm2 and 95.52 %, respectively, which was closely related to the adsorption process of Zn-N-CDs at solution/metal interface. In the meantime, the analysis of corrosion products, adsorption models, and molecular dynamics (MD) models confirmed the physical and chemical adsorption mechanism. Moreover, the obtained Zn-N-CDs also exhibited good antibacterial ability, with the maximum inhibition rate of 90 % and 89 % for Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Zn/N
Environmental-friendly
Adsorption behavior
Corrosion protection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structural characterization of inhibitor (a) FTIR; (b) UV–Vis; (c) C1s, (d) N1s and (e) O1s of undoped inhibitor; (f) C1s, (g) N1s, (h) O1s and (i) Zn2p of doped inhibitor.
(a) TEM and (a, c, e, g) AFM images of undoped inhibitor (c, e, g); (b) TEM and (d, f, h) AFM image of doped inhibitor.
The results of plate counting method after adding inhibitor.
The bacteria inhibition rates at different cultivation times.
EIS results of (a, b) Zn0 %-N-CDs, (c, d) Zn5 %-N-CDs, (e, f) Zn10 %-N-CDs and (g, h) Zn20 %-N-CDs.
Tafel curves of (a) Zn0 %-N-CDs, (b) Zn5 %-N-CDs, (c) Zn10 %-N-CDs and (d) Zn20 %-N-CDs.
3D morphology (a-g) and surface roughness (h) of metal surface at different conditions.
2D morphology and EDS analysis of metal surface at different conditions.
XPS results containing (a) C1s, (b) N1s, (c) Zn2p3/2 and (d) Cu2p3/2 of metal surface after adding Zn-N-CDs solution.
The corrosion rate of metal at different conditions containing (a) Zn/N ratio, (b) soaking time and concentration.
The C∼C/θ curve for (a) Zn0 %-N-CDs, (b) Zn5 %-N-CDs, (c) Zn10 %-N-CDs, (d) Zn20 %-N-CDs; (e) Kabs; (f) ΔG.
Equilibrium adsorption configuration of ammonium citrate (a, b, c), zinc gluconate (d, e, f) and Zn-N-CDs (g, h, i) on Cu (111) surface from front, side and vertical view.

head.ce:title: Zinc and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as green and efficient anticorrosion and antibacterial inhibitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated Ganzhou Hospital, Jiangxi Medical College

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, 341000, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bFaculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, 341000, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Prevention and Treatment of Cardiovascular and Cerebrovascular Diseases of Ministry of Education, Gannan Medical University, Ganzhou 341000, Jiangxi Province, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Prevention and Treatment of Cardiovascular and Cerebrovascular Diseases of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cKey Laboratory of Prevention and Treatment of Cardiovascular and Cerebrovascular Diseases of Ministry of Education, Gannan Medical University, Ganzhou 341000, Jiangxi Province, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green and efficient method was used to synthesize zinc and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Zn-N-CDs) using ammonium citrate and zinc gluconate as raw materials. A systematic and in-depth study was conducted on the microstructure and protective behaviors of Zn-N-CDs. Results revealed that the impedance modulus (|Z|0.01 Hz) and inhibition efficiency (IE) showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing with the increase of Zn/N ratio, achieving the highest values of 4727 Ω cm2 and 95.52 %, respectively, which was closely related to the adsorption process of Zn-N-CDs at solution/metal interface. In the meantime, the analysis of corrosion products, adsorption models, and molecular dynamics (MD) models confirmed the physical and chemical adsorption mechanism. Moreover, the obtained Zn-N-CDs also exhibited good antibacterial ability, with the maximum inhibition rate of 90 % and 89 % for Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology exhibits significant advantage in many fields, including anti-corrosion and biomedical applications [1–4]. Carbon dots (CDs) with zero dimensional nanostructured materials have attracted considerable attention in the field of inhibitors due to their excellent performances and potential applications [5,6]. In the meantime, Due to their excellent optical performances, good water solubility, super stability, environmental friendliness, and ideal biocompatibility, they have become an important material in many fields such as medical diagnostics, anticorrosion, and bioimaging [7–10]. On the basis of carbon dots, doping with metal or non-metal elements can further improve their protection ability [11–13].

In the terms of non-metallic doping, Zhao’s team [14] prepared a kind of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using ammonium citrate as raw material through high temperature pyrolysis method, and found that it exhibited excellent protective effect on metal in corrosive environments. Huang et al. [15] obtained a good water soluble inhibitor of NCDs through citric acid, and showed that it presented excellent luminescent properties and great potential in the field of bacterial inhibition. Liu et al. [16] obtained a high efficiency N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) by a cost-effective method, and discovered that the synergistic effect of N and S could significantly improve the low-frequency impedance of metals in corrosive environments and reduce corrosion current density (icorr). Ahmed et al. [17] created N and Si co-doped carbon dots (N, Si-CDs) using di ammonium hydrogen citrate and ammonia as raw materials, and illustrated that the inhibition efficiency of NCDs was greatly enhanced by doping an appropriate amount of Si atoms.

In case of metallic doping, Zeng et al. [18] developed a Ce-doped carbon dots by hydrothermal synthesis, and pointed out that the IE reached up to 98 % for copper when 200 mg/L of inhibitor was added to 1 M HCl solution. Tang et al. [19] obtained a Ce-doped carbon dots using l-histidine and cerium nitrate hexahydrate as raw materials through hydrothermal method, and suggested that the obtained not only revealed super corrosion inhibition performance, but also presented good antibacterial ability. Subash et al. [20] obtained a Cu-doped carbon dots as effective inhibitor for low carbon steel, and showed that the formation of Cu-containing compounds on mild steel surface was beneficial for inhibiting corrosion.

This study presented an innovative approach to strengthen protection activity through the synthesis of Zn-N-CDs by incorporating metallic Zn and nonmetallic N. Among them, Zn came from zinc gluconate, N originated from ammonium citrate. Microstructure, morphology, size, bonding status and composition characterization of inhibitor contained Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Ultraviolet-visible adsorption spectroscopy (UV–Vis), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscope (TEM), atomic force microscope (AFM). Plate coating method was used for evaluating the antibacterial behaviors of inhibitor. Meanwhile, the corrosion protection behavior of inhibitor for metal was analyzed by impedance modulus, inhibition efficiency and corrosion rate. Moreover, the molecular dynamics simulation calculation was select for illustrating the protection mechanism of inhibitor.

Ammonium citrate, zinc gluconate, anhydrous ethanol, sodium chloride, etc. were obtained from the market. All chemicals were utilized as received without further purification, and distilled water was prepared in the laboratory.

The synthesis process of inhibitor was presented in Fig. 1 . Based on experimental plan, the qualities of ammonium citrate and zinc gluconate were measured using a precision electronic scale. Then, zinc gluconate (0.1, 0.2 and 0.4 g) and ammonium citrate (2.0 g) were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water and then ultrasonicated for 15 mins. The obtained mixture was transferred to high pressure reactor at 473 K for 4 h. After that, the resulting solution was first filtered through qualitative filter paper and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 mins to remove particulate matter. Finally, the solution was collected and dried in a vacuum oven at 343 K for 24 h. The undoped sample (without zinc gluconate addition) was synthesized under identical experimental conditions. For convenience, these specimens were marked sequentially as Zn0 %-N-CDs, Zn5 %-N-CDs, Zn10 %-N-CDs, Zn20 %-N-CDs, respectively.

Composition of the undoped and doped inhibitors was characterized through FTIR (NICOLET 6700) and UV–vis (UV-2550). Bonding status and composition of the undoped and doped inhibitors were characterized by XPS (PHI 5000 VersaProbe). Morphology and size of the undoped and doped inhibitors were characterized by TEM (JEM-2100F) and AFM (Bruker Dimension Edge). Plate coating method was used for evaluating the antibacterial activity of undoped and doped inhibitors. Weight loss measurement was chosen to evaluate the corrosion inhibition abilities of the undoped and doped inhibitors. Corrosion morphology and surface roughness of metal in different environments were acquired through scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS Sigma) and 3D profilometer (NanoMap 500LS). Component of the adsorption film on metal surface was analyzed by XPS (PHI 5000 VersaProbe).

Electrochemical measurements were performed using a CHI660E workstation with a standard three-electrode configuration in 3.5 % NaCl solution. The setup consisted of a platinum wire mesh counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode, and a copper working electrode. All tests were carried out at a stable temperature of 293 K and a relative humidity of 65 %. The protective behavior was quantitatively assessed using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (Tafel curve). Prior to each test, the open circuit potential (OCP) was monitored for achieving a steady-state potential. Bode diagram and Nyquist diagram were obtained at a frequency range from 10–2 Hz to 105 Hz with a AC amplitude of ±5 mV. Tafel test was executed at −0.25 V∼ +0.25 V vs. OCP (potential range) and 1 mV/s (scanning rate). After that, the data analysis was conducted using ZSimpWin software.

Molecular dynamics model was selected for analyzing the statistical adsorption behavior of inhibitor molecule on metal surface. All molecular dynamics simulations and structural optimizations were performed in Materials Studio. Subsequently, the adsorption model of inhibitor molecule on metal surface in 3.5 % NaCl solution was created in Amorphous Cell module. After that, Cu (111) model with the thickness of 6 layers was created for analysis. The simulated system comprised 500 water molecules, 10 sodium ions, 10 chloride ions and 1 inhibitor molecule. The model was first optimized by Smart Algorithm and then simulated through a dynamical equilibrium simulation process. The adsorption behavior of Zn-N-CDs was strengthened by COMPASS force field with the circumstances of 298.0 K and 1.0 fs per step for 100 ps by NVT.

Fig. 2
a showed the FTIR spectrum of both undoped and doped inhibitors. For undoped inhibitor, several peaks around 1717, 1602, 1445, 1370, and 1399 cm-1 were detected, which corresponded to the vibration of CO, CC, C–N , C–OH, and C–O–C, respectively [21,22]. Meanwhile, a weak peak around at 3420 cm-1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of -COOH and -NH2, and the stretching vibration of C–H was observed around at 2930 cm-1. After adding Zn, an additional band around at 638 cm-1 was found, confirming the existence of Zn-O/Zn-N groups. This suggested the successful preparation of Zn and N co-doped carbon dots [23]. Fig. 2 b revealed the UV–Vis spectrum of both undoped and doped inhibitor. The UV absorption spectrum of undoped inhibitor exhibited one distinct peak around at 338 nm, while the position of the peak transferred to 336 nm in doped inhibitor, implying that the electron transition was affected by doping Zn.

Fig. 2 c–i presented the XPS spectra of both undoped and doped inhibitors. In the term of undoped inhibitor, the peaks around at 284.6, 286.5, and 289.0 eV were related to CC, CC/CN, and CO bonds in C1s spectrum, respectively [24]. The N1s spectrum revealed two distinct peaks, which attributed to NC bond (400.3 eV) and NH bond (398.6 eV), respectively [25]. The O1s spectrum was divided into two peaks around at 530.9 and 532.2 eV, which corresponded to OC and OC bonds, respectively [26]. For doped inhibitor, the N1s and C1s spectra were similar to those of undoped inhibitor. The O1s spectrum could be resolved into two peaks, caused by OC (531.2 eV) and OC (532.2 eV). The Zn2p spectrum revealed two distinct peaks, which attributed to Zn2+ [27].

Fig. 3 displayed the TEM and AFM morphologies of both undoped and doped inhibitors. From the result, both inhibitors maintained quasi-spherical shapes and were monodispersed without apparent aggregation. By analysis, the size distribution of both inhibitors was about 1–6 nm. Among them, the main size of undoped inhibitor was concentrated in 3–4 nm, while the value of doped inhibitor was about 4–5 nm, suggesting that the doping of Zn could slightly increase the size of inhibitor (Fig. 3 a and b). The similar result was also observed in the AFM. The height of undoped inhibitor was lower than that of doped inhibitor (Fig. 3 c–h).

Escherichia coli and S. aureus were selected as the research object, the results of plate counting method after adding inhibitor were shown in Fig. 4 . At the blank condition, both bacteria covered most of the area of culture dish, and some local aggregations were observed, suggesting that the bacteria presented a high reproductive ability under this condition. After adding Zn0 %-N-CDs, the bacteria still covered most of the culture dish, but the aggregation phenomenon disappeared. With the increase of Zn addition amount, the distribution area of bacteria was significantly shrunk, and the distribution density was sharply decreased, implying a great improvement in antibacterial activity. When the amount of zinc gluconate exceeded 10 %, the growth of bacteria tended to increase again. This phenomenon indicated that appropriate Zn doping could enhance the antibacterial activity of inhibitor.

After adding inhibitors, the bacteria inhibition rates at various cultivation times were presented in Fig. 5 . For two types of bacteria, the trend of these curves was similar and divided into two stages. With the initial addition of inhibitors, the bacteria inhibition rate sharply increased and then entered a slow increasing stage. At the same conditions, the bacteria inhibition rate increased first and then decreased, obtaining the largest inhibition rate at Zn10 %-N-CDs. In this case, the bacteria inhibition rates for E. coli and S. aureus were about 90 % and 89 %, respectively, which exhibited a certain potential in antibacterial agent. Meanwhile, by comparison, the inhibition action of Zn-N-CDs for E. coli was slightly stronger than that for S. aureus.

The impedance spectra for metal in 3.5 % NaCl solution with and without inhibitor at room temperature were exhibited as Nyquist and Bode plots in Fig. 6 . The capacitance arc radius (abbreviated as arc radius) and low frequency impedance modulus (abbreviated as impedance value) were important parameters for evaluating corrosion resistance [28]. In the blank condition, the arc radius and impedance value both minimized among all conditions, suggesting severe corrosion. After the addition of Zn0 %-N-CDs, the arc radius and impedance value of metal were significantly magnified. Meanwhile, the increase in impedance was directly related to inhibitor addition amount. As the addition amount of inhibitor increased, the impedance value of metal in 3.5 % NaCl solution showed an increasing trend. Specifically, as the Zn0 %-N-CDs addition amount changed from 50 to 200 mg/L, the impedance value increased from 1666 to 3023 Ω cm2. After doping Zn, the arc radius and impedance value were increased to some extent. At the condition of 200 mg/L Zn5 %-N-CDs, the impedance value was around 3560 Ω cm2, which was 17.8 % higher in comparison to Zn0 %-N-CDs. When the addition amount of zinc gluconate increased to 10 %, the impedance value was greatly increased. When the addition amount of Zn10 %-N-CDs increased (50–200 mg/L), the impedance value increased from 3902 to 4727 Ω cm2. Compared with blank condition, the impedance value was increased by 6–7.5 times. However, the impedance value exhibited a decreasing trend as the amount of zinc gluconate continued to increase, implying that the protection ability of Zn10 %-N-CDs reached the peak.

Fig. 7 presented the Tafel curves of the inhibitor with different zinc gluconate doping ratios (0, 5 %, 10 % and 20 %) and various addition amounts (0, 50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L). By calculation, the relevant parameters were obtained and exhibited in Table 1 . Among them, the corrosion potential (Ecorr) and icorr were fitted in the linear region of Tafel curve. The value of surface coverage (θ) and IE was calculated through Eqs. (1) and (2) [29,30]:
(1)
q =
(
i
b l a n k
−
i
c o r r
)
/
i
b l a n k
(2)
I E =
(
i
b l a n k
−
i
c o r r
)
/
i
b l a n k
* 100 %

The meanings of iblank and icorr values were defined in our previous work [19]. A lower icorr value corresponded to decreased corrosion rate and enhanced resistance to corrosion. By observation, the icorr value in the blank condition was the highest, corresponding to the weakest resistance to corrosion. After introducing Zn0 %-N-CDs into the blank solution, the icorr value was significantly reduced. At the same time, the decrease of icorr value was directly related to the inhibitor addition amount. With the increase of addition amount, the icorr value displayed a decreasing trend. For example, as the addition amount of Zn0 %-N-CDs increased, the icorr value changed from 11.3 to 6.12 μA/cm2. After doping Zn, the icorr value were decreased to a certain extent. At 200 mg/L of Zn5 %-N-CDs, the icorr value was about 4.99 μA/cm2, which was 18.5 % lower than that of Zn0 %-N-CDs. When the zinc gluconate increased to 10 %, the icorr value was greatly reduced. When the amount of addition increased (50–200 mg/L), the icorr value dropped from 5.29 to 1.63 μA/cm2. Compared with the blank condition, the icorr value was decreased by 85 %−96 %. Nevertheless, with the further increase of zinc gluconate amount, the icorr value presented a rebound trend, implying that the corrosion resistance began to weak.

A similar trend was observed for both θ and IE. With the addition of inhibitors, both θ and IE values exhibited a gradual increase. In case of Zn0 %-N-CDs, when the amount of addition increased (50–200 mg/L), the θ and IE values increased from 0.72 to 72.06 % to 0.83 and 83.19 %, respectively. After doping Zn, the θ and IE showed a certain degree of improvement. Meanwhile, as the addition amount of zinc gluconate increased, both values initially rose and then declined, reaching the highest at the condition of Zn10 %-N-CDs. In this case, the θ and IE values were up to 0.96 and 95.52 % at condition of 200 mg/L, which was higher than that in some previous studies [31–35]. A further increase in zinc gluconate led to the decrease of θ and IE values, suggesting that the adsorption behavior of inhibitor was strongly dependent on the addition amount of Zn.

3D morphology after corrosion provided important information on surface characteristics of metal at different conditions, which was shown in Fig. 8 . Initially, the metal surface appeared obvious furrows and protrusions after soaking in the corrosive environment. Through measurement, the surface roughness (Ra) of metal in the blank solution was high (0.334 μm). By introducing Zn0 %-N-CDs inhibitor, the corrosion on surface was mildly inhibited, the Ra was slightly reduced. However, the metal surface became relatively flat after the addition of Zn-N-CDs, with only a few raised areas. This observation sufficiently demonstrated the effective corrosion inhibition ability of Zn-N-CDs. At the conditions of 24 h and 100 mg/L, the Ra exhibited a trend of decreasing first and then increasing as the zinc gluconate increased, reaching the peak value of 0.239 μm at Zn10 %-N-CDs. Meanwhile, under the aforementioned conditions, increasing the addition amount of Zn10 %-N-CDs to 200 mg/L resulted in a flat metal surface with no obvious protrusions, accompanied by a continuous decrease in Ra, which clearly demonstrated its superior corrosion inhibition performance. Moreover, reducing the soaking time resulted in localized protrusions on the metal surface, leading to a slight increase in surface roughness.

2D morphology and EDS analysis could provide valuable insights into the surface morphology (Fig. 9
). The surface of sample after soaking in the blank solution exhibited complex features, including cracking, oxidation, peeling, and other phenomena. The introduction of Zn0 %-N-CDs reduced corrosion signs to some extent. However, the persistent presence of numerous surface protrusions indicated suboptimal corrosion resistance. When immersed in Zn-N-CDs condition, the metal surface exhibited relatively smooth and uniform morphology, implying significant corrosion inhibition. Among them, the corrosion degree of metal surface in Zn10 %-N-CDs environment was the slightest, which was close related to the protective layer caused by inhibitor. By EDS analysis, several elements such Zn, N, C, O, Na and Cl were detected on metal surface. Thereinto, Zn and N were from inhibitor, Na and Cl came from solution environment, the existence of O might lead to oxidation reaction. Thus, the protective layer might include inhibitor and oxide films. Furthermore, the formation of cracks and pits on the metal substrate was effectively suppressed by increasing Zn-N-CDs addition amount, resulting in comprehensive surface protection. Conversely, the decrease of soaking time might exacerbate the occurrence of corrosion. Appropriate soaking time and addition amount could achieve optimal protection for metals.

Fig. 10 was the XPS result of corrosion products on metal surface after corrosion testing. The C1s, N1s, Zn2p3/2, Cu2p3/2 peaks were detected after the metal immersed in the corrosion solution, indicating that the Zn-N-CDs adsorbed at interface, which was consistent with the result of SEM-EDS test. For C1s spectrum, three peaks around at 284.6, 285.9 and 288.6 eV were regarded as CC, CC/CN and CO bonds, respectively [24]. Meanwhile, the N1s peak was located at 399.3 and 400.4 eV, which was related to NCu and NC bonds [36], implying the interaction between Zn-N-CDs and metal surface. In addition, the Zn2p3/2 spectrum could be resolved into two peaks at 1021.6 and 1022.5 eV, corresponding to Zn-N and Zn-O bonds, which confirmed the formation of Zn-containing oxides at the interface [37]. Finally, two peaks around at 933.2 and 932.9 eV in the Cu2p3/2 deconvolution could be attributed to Cu-O and Cu-Cl bonds, respectively [38,39]. Thus, the Cl- from the solution also reacted with the metal matrix to form compounds (Cu-Cl) on the metal surface, which could inhibit the further penetration of corrosive media to some extent. From the above corrosion components, it could be seen that the protection of Zn-N-CDs inhibitor mainly included two parts (chemical adsorption and physical adsorption). On the one hand, the chemical adsorption was ascribed to the heteroatom of N, which could easily fill in the unoccupied 3D orbitals outside the Cu atoms because of the lone pair electrons, and then formed a coordinate bond between heteroatom and Cu. On the other hand, the physical adsorption was attributed to the agglomeration effect of aromatic heterocyclic and the formation of Zn-containing passive film on the surface of Cu. The oxidation reaction between Zn and ambient oxygen generated a compact oxide film that adsorbed onto the Cu substrate surface, further improving the protective performance.

The weight loss of metal was evaluated in 3.5 % NaCl solution with and without inhibitor at room temperature, the result was presented in Fig. 11 . The calculation equation was as follows:
(3)
V
C R
=
(
W 1
−
W 2
)
/ T

Among them, W1 represented the sample quality before soaking test (g), W2 was the sample quality after soaking test (g), T denoted the soaking time (h). In 3.5 % NaCl solution, the initial corrosion rate of metal was around 6 × 10–5 g/h. After adding inhibitor, the corrosion rate decreased to various degrees. As the addition amount of Zn increased, the corrosion rate of metal firstly dropped and then rebounded, achieving the smallest value at Zn10 %-N-CDs. In this case, the corrosion rate was sharply reduced in comparison to the blank condition. This phenomenon could be attributed to the enhanced surface coverage of metal facilitated by inhibitor adsorption. As the addition amount of Zn10 %-N-CDs decreased to 50 mg/L, the corrosion rate of metal increased to 1.3 × 10–5 g/h, representing a two-fold increase compared to the 200 mg/L condition. This might be due to the insufficient adsorption of Zn10 %-N-CDs on the adsorption sites of metal surface. A similar trend was observed with variations in soaking time. When the soaking time shortened to 12 h, the corrosion rate was increased by nearly 50 %, indicating that the effective adsorption of inhibitor need to be completed within sufficient time.

Langmuir adsorption isotherm was preliminarily selected to explore the interaction mechanism between inhibitor and metal surface, which was displayed in Fig. 12 . Among them, the adsorption free energy (ΔG) was a key parameter to reveal the adsorption mechanism, which could be calculated through Eqs. (4) and (5) [40,41]:
(4)
C θ
= C +
1
K ads
(5)
Δ
G = − RTln
(
1000
K ads
)

The connotations of C, θ, Kads, ΔG, R and T could be inferred from the results of previous study [9]. From the result, all fitting degrees (R2) were nearly 1, implying that the adsorption process of all inhibitors complied with the isothermal adsorption model. In the meantime, the change of Kads and ΔG showed a similar trend. With the increase of Zn addition amount, both values first increased and then decreased, achieving the largest values at Zn10 %-N-CDs. In this case, the inhibitor exhibited strongest adsorption onto metal surfaces. Moreover, all ΔG values were belong to −20 ∼ −40 kJ·mol-1, indicating that the function essence of all inhibitors was mixed adsorption [42]. Thereinto, the Zn10 %-N-CDs exhibited the highest probability of interfacial interactions among all samples according to thermodynamic calculations.

The adsorption behaviors of ammonium citrate, zinc gluconate and Zn-N-CDs on copper surface were deeply investigated by molecular dynamics simulation, as shown in Fig. 13 . In the case of ammonium citrate, it formed a certain angle with the metal interface and then distributed in a warped configuration. This warping might allow corrosive medium to penetrate from the side of inhibitor into the distorted region, thereby accelerating corrosion. Different to ammonium citrate, the adsorption of zinc gluconate was mainly divided into two parts. A gluconate ion was oriented perpendicularly at the interface, failing to provide significant inhibition. In contrast, another gluconate ion adopted a parallel orientation, effectively blocking the penetration of corrosive medium. In terms of Zn-N-CDs, it primarily adsorbed onto metal surfaces in a parallel orientation, with the main adsorption active site located at the position of N atom. Compared with ammonium citrate and zinc gluconate, the obtained Zn-N-CDs exhibited more extensive and tighter adsorption on metal surfaces, offering broader protection against corrosion. The adsorption energy (Ebinding) could be used to illustrate the magnitude of adsorption strength. First, the negative values indicated stable adsorption on metal surfaces. Second, the shift of adsorption energy toward more negative values indicated a stronger inhibitory effect. Through calculation, the Ebinding of ammonium citrate, zinc gluconate and Zn-N-CDs was −11.85, −11.15 and −56.92 kcal/mol, respectively. This suggested that the interface interaction of Zn-N-CDs was the strongest, revealing the best protection effect for metal.

In this work, a series of environmental-friendly and functional carbon dots doped with metallic zinc and non-metallic nitrogen were prepared. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, polarization curve and weight loss measurements demonstrated that the addition of Zn-N-CDs significantly reduced the corrosion rate of metal. Meanwhile, the inhibition efficiency initially increased and then decreased with increasing Zn/N ratio, reaching a maximum of 95.52 % for Zn10 %-N-CDs. Corrosion product and Langmuir adsorption isotherm analysis revealed that Zn-N-CDs chemically adsorbed onto the copper surface through Zn-O and NCu bonds, facilitating the formation of a protective film that effectively suppressed corrosion. Molecular simulation calculation indicated that Zn-N-CDs exhibited a more negative adsorption energy, promoting their stable adsorption on the metal surface. Additionally, Zn-N-CDs displayed strong antibacterial activity, with an inhibition rate of nearly 90 % against both E. coli and S. aureus.

Zhiji Tang: Writing – original draft, Resources. Weilong Huang: Investigation, Supervision. Changgui Guo: Resources, Investigation. Huanglian Zhong: Investigation. Lin Liu: Investigation. Pengfei Zhang: Investigation. Tianhui Tan: Investigation. Mingang Zou: Resources. Shiyu Zheng: Methodology. Xinyun Ye: Resources, Investigation. Y.W. Ye: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation. Qiuhua Jiang: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was sponsored by Jiangxi Provincial Health Technology Project; the Joint Funds of the Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No 20244BAB28020 and No 20244BAB28036; Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 20224BAB206069); National Nature Science Foundation of China (grant No 52461016); Jiangxi Province High-level and High-skilled Leading Talents Training Project (2021); Ganzhou Science and Technology Plan Project (2023LNS17423 and GZ2024YLJ004).
